steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: bowel obstruction, colonic mass/malignancy, large bowel ileus (given constipation and distension)
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the recurrent lower abdominal pain represents complicated or uncomplicated diverticulitis (or an alternative structural cause such as obstruction or mass), and the anatomic extent/severity of any colonic pathology
ensemble.expected_finding: Pericolonic fat stranding and/or colonic wall thickening consistent with diverticulitis, or alternatively evidence of bowel obstruction, abscess, perforation, or a colonic mass
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has known diverticulosis, recurrent lower abdominal pain that partially responded to antibiotics, constipation, a soft but distended abdomen with suprapubic tenderness, mild leukocytosis, and no prior cross-sectional imaging — CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the standard first-line modality to characterize diverticulitis severity and exclude competing diagnoses.
grounding: History: 'She has known diverticulosis, noted on colonoscopy ___ years ago'
History: 'Her abdominal pain had improved with antibiotics, but recurred this past week'
History: 'Also notes constipation for the past couple of days'
Physical examination: 'Soft, distended, mild tenderness to palpation to suprapubic area, no rebound/guarding'
Laboratory: 'White Blood Cells: 10.5 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)'
Laboratory: 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 7.29 K/uL (ref 1.6-6.1)'
Laboratory: 'Lipase: 17.0 IU/L' — effectively excludes pancreatitis
Imaging already resulted BEFORE this decision point: none
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a compelling story for recurrent diverticulitis — known diverticulosis, episodic lower abdominal pain, partial antibiotic response, and now recurrence with constipation and a distended abdomen — but I have no cross-sectional imaging to confirm the diagnosis, grade severity, or exclude complications such as abscess, perforation, or fistula. The mild leukocytosis and suprapubic tenderness without peritoneal signs leave open the possibility of a colonic mass or obstruction driving the presentation. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the most direct way to anatomically characterize the colon, identify pericolonic inflammation or free air, and rule out alternative structural pathology before committing to a treatment plan.

ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is anatomic evidence of active diverticulitis (pericolic fat stranding, abscess, perforation) versus an alternative structural cause of recurrent lower abdominal pain and constipation, which cannot be determined from history, exam, and labs alone
ensemble.expected_finding: Pericolic fat stranding with thickening of the sigmoid or descending colon wall consistent with acute diverticulitis, or alternatively a colonic mass, obstruction, or abscess explaining the recurrent pain and antibiotic-responsive but relapsing course
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has known diverticulosis, a prior antibiotic-responsive episode suggesting diverticulitis, now recurrent lower abdominal pain with constipation and mild leukocytosis, making CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast the standard first-line imaging to confirm diverticulitis, assess for complications (abscess, perforation), and exclude alternative diagnoses such as colonic malignancy.
